text: Bitterness. Instead of comfort, he only meets with insult from his friends. He therefore appeals to God, (Worthington) but with fear. (Calmet)<hr>Scourge, is not in Hebrew. (Menochius)<hr>But it explains the meaning of "my hand," (Haydock) or the heavy chastisement (St. Gregory) which I endure. (Menochius)
Nor. Protestants, "No, but he would put strength in me." He would enable me to stand my trial. (Haydock)<hr>He would lay no falsehood to my charge.

Victory. Before such a judge I should hope to be acquitted. Job must have been well convinced of his innocence. For woe to man, if God treat him according to the rigour of his justice! (Calmet)<hr>Septuagint, "For truth and reproof are with him. But, oh! that he would bring my judgment to an end." (Haydock)
East, or Hebrew, "before....if behind I cannot perceive him." (Calmet)<hr>Where, then, shall I find this just judge? ver. 3. (Haydock)

Right, southward.<hr>See. The southern part of the heavens, or the antarctic stars would not be discerned in Idumea, Job 9:9. (Calmet)<hr>Job speaks in a human manner, as he was not ignorant of God's immensity. (Menochius)
Fire. They had not a stone, like the Greeks, to try gold. (Calmet)

Lips, given to Noe, (Genesis 9:5.) or to Moses. This work was written by a Jew, who dresses up the sentiments of Job in his own style. The holy man was also acquainted with the Mosaic dispensation, though he was not bound to observe the ceremonial part of it. (Calmet)<hr>His words may, however, be verified, though he speak only of the natural law, or of that which was preserved by tradition of the patriarchs, as both proceeded from God. (Haydock)<hr>Hid. Protestants, "esteemed....more than my necessary food." (Haydock)<hr>Septuagint agree with the Vulgate. The ancients placed in their bosom what they greatly esteemed. The Turks still use it as a pocket. (Thevenot xxii.) (Calmet)
Alone. Self-existent. Hebrew, "in one thing," or resolution. He is immutable: all I could say would avail nothing. (Calmet)

With him, to punish me afresh. Hebrew, "when he shall have appointed me my portion, he shall still have many such." He may treat me as a slave, allowing me only a certain portion of meat, Proverbs 30:8., and 31:15. (Calmet)
Face. My afflictions have not yet taken away my life, as might have been expected. (Haydock)<hr>I am less affected with my miseries, than with the dread of God's presence, ver. 15, 16. (Calmet)
